text: Last class you discussed a contract and what could go wrong.
Today the service goes down, and you must explain what happened.

At eleven p.m., the payment service was returning errors.
Customers were trying to check out when the system failed.
When the engineer joined, the database was running out of memory.
While the team was fixing the outage, I updated the status page.
Good work last night, Rafael. The war room was calm. Can you tell me what you were seeing when the incident started?
Customers were trying to check out, but the service was returning errors. We set the severity to one right away.
I checked the logs. While the team was fixing the outage, I saw that a memory alert was firing twenty minutes before we declared the incident.
So we were already in trouble before we noticed. We remediated the problem fast, but the disruption was longer than it needed to be.
Exactly. Your message on the status page was clear and honest, so customers stayed calm. The fix was fine; detection is the real lesson.
Agreed. Let us run the postmortem tomorrow and add a better alert as the first action item, so we catch it while it is small.
We are investigating an incident with the payment service.
We opened a war room and set the severity to one.
We remediated the issue, and the outage is now over.
We will run a postmortem to prevent this disruption next time.

Hi team, this is Rafael with a quick update on last night's incident. At about eleven p.m., while most of us were sleeping, the payment service went down. Customers were trying to check out, but the system was returning errors, so we opened a war room and declared a sev-one. When the on-call engineer joined, the database was already running out of memory. We worked together to remediate the problem: we restarted the service, and the outage ended at around midnight. The disruption lasted about an hour, and it affected roughly two thousand customers. I have updated the status page and posted an apology. Tomorrow we will run a postmortem to understand the root cause and prevent it next time. Thank you for staying calm while we were fixing it, and please send me any details you noticed during the incident.

Hi Rafael, this is Marina with a short follow-up before the postmortem tomorrow. I reviewed the logs from last night. While the team was restarting the service, I noticed that the memory alert had been firing for twenty minutes before we declared the incident. So the real lesson is about detection, not the fix. The severity was correct, the war room worked well, and you communicated clearly on the status page. My only suggestion is this: if the alert was so loud, we should have escalated sooner. For the postmortem, I will prepare a timeline of the outage, the customer impact, and three action items to reduce the disruption next time. The most important one is a better alert, so we catch the problem while it is small. See you tomorrow, and thank you for leading the response so calmly.

First, confirm the incident is real and check how many customers it is affecting. Next, open a war room and set the severity, so everyone knows how urgent it is. Then, work with the on-call engineer to remediate the problem and end the outage. After that, update the status page and post a clear, honest message for customers. Finally, run a postmortem to find the root cause, and write action items so the same disruption does not happen again.
